1.display: They are exactly the same shape and size (one can be moved, turned, or flipped onto the other exactly).

6.display: False — same angles only proves the triangles are similar, not congruent (one could be bigger).

11.display: No — they have the same angles but different sizes, so they are similar, not congruent.

13.display: RHS proves congruence; the missing side is √(10² − 6²) = √64 = 8 cm.

14.display: Congruent shapes are identical in shape AND size; similar shapes have the same shape (equal angles, proportional sides) but can be different sizes.

15.display: Sometimes — triangles can share a perimeter with different side lengths (not congruent), or happen to have identical sides (congruent).

16.display: ASA (the 7 cm side is included between the 90° and 40° angles).

17.display: No — with two sides and a non-included angle, two different triangles can often be drawn, so SSA does not reliably prove congruence.

18.display: A reflection is a rigid transformation — it keeps all lengths and angles the same, so the image is always congruent to the original.

19.display: Example: two equilateral triangles both have angles 60°, 60°, 60° but can have different side lengths (e.g. 2 cm and 5 cm) — same shape, different size, so not congruent.

20.display: No — impossible. Equal legs of 5 cm and a base angle of 70° fix the whole triangle (SAS), so the base length must also be the same; the two triangles would have to be congruent.

22.display: Missing side = √(13² − 5²) = √144 = 12 m for both; RHS proves they are congruent.

23.display: SAS (AB = DE, AC = DF, and the included angle at A equals the included angle at D) — yes, the triangles are congruent.

24.display: ASA — two matching angles (55°, 65°) with the included side (40 m) proves the two survey triangles are identical.

25.display: No — the third sides differ (10 cm vs 8 cm), so the triangles are not congruent even though two pairs of sides match.

26.display: 50 cm (a 30-40-50 triangle, which is the 3-4-5 Pythagorean triple scaled ×10).

27.display: In a right-angled triangle, once you know the right angle, hypotenuse, and one other side, the third side is fixed by Pythagoras' theorem — so matching those three facts is really just matching all three sides (SSS) in disguise.

28.display: Example: a triangle with base 8 cm and height 3 cm, and another with base 6 cm and height 4 cm — both have area 12 cm² but different shapes, so equal area alone cannot prove congruence.

30.display: Your own pair of triangles with side/angle measurements, correctly naming the congruence rule (SSS, SAS, ASA, RHS) that applies, or explaining that none apply.
